title: USD Identifier Scanner Capability
purpose: Define the reusable semantic boundary for recognizing USD identifiers embedded inside larger grammars.

included: single identifier span scanning from an offset
single identifier continuation code point scanning from an offset
namespaced property names as colon-separated identifier components
keywordless identifier validation where the caller requests it
excluded: USDA string, number, asset, or comment lexing

model.IdentifierScanner: Target-native capability that consumes an identifier or identifier continuation span from caller-owned text and returns the next offset or a structured failure.
model.IdentifierSpan: A non-owning or owning view of the matched source span. The contract does not require string allocation.
model.NameMode: Caller-selected mode such as identifier component, namespaced property name, or keywordless identifier. The provider owns the name validity rules for each exposed mode.

input.offset: starting offset in target-native text units at a code point boundary
input.mode: identifier component or other provider-defined name mode

output.end: offset immediately after the matched identifier on success
output.span: matched source span, view, or equivalent target-native representation

input.offset: starting offset in target-native text units at a code point boundary

output.end: offset immediately after one valid identifier-continuation code point

input.mode: identifier component, namespaced property name, or keywordless identifier

output.canonical: validated identifier text or provider-native validated name value

consumption_rules.default: A dependent skill that consumes this capability must delegate identifier recognition and UTF-8/XID validity to the provider rather than reimplementing it locally.
allowed_consumer_logic: zero-allocation or view-based glue around the provider capability
forbidden_consumer_logic: local identifier validity rules that can diverge from the provider

performance_exception.meaning: A consumer may use a target-local fast path only when the provider interface would violate the consumer's declared performance contract.
requirements: the exception is declared in the graph or target report
the local fast path remains semantically bound to this provider contract
the consumer passes the provider conformance tests for the consumed subset
the consumer passes its own conformance tests and performance targets
the target documents why the provider interface is insufficient before forking the local fast path
preference_order: improve the provider capability API so the consumer can use it directly
use a declared target-local fast path as a last resort
